name: Platform-neutral procurement requirements for Danish digital identity
consequences: Direct effect: public sector procurement shifts to evaluate solutions based on platform-neutral capabilities rather than OS-specific trust signals. Downstream, improves resilience by expanding potential vendors and moving away from reliance on single platform controls, but may slow procurement cycles and increase compliance burdens for agencies during pilot phases.
options: Codify platform-neutral access criteria into Danish public-sector procurement templates and contract terms, basing eligibility on open standards rather than platform-specific trust signals.
Launch cross-department procurement pilots that test vendor capabilities against fallback authentication requirements using open standards.
Institute a periodic procurement-review mechanism to assess vendor compliance with platform neutrality and adapt criteria as standards evolve.
review: Core tension centers on balancing speed of procurement with a robust, platform-neutral baseline; the weakness is that the options do not specify a concrete sunset plan for legacy MitID interfaces or a risk-based migration path.

name: AltID interoperability and procurement language at NSIS/eIDAS2 level
consequences: Direct effect: pushes the trust framework toward portable wallet interoperability and reduces platform lock-in; downstream, it may trigger complexity in certification regimes and longer vendor negotiations.
options: Advocate for formal AltID interoperability benchmarks within NSIS and eIDAS2 that require platform-neutral fallbacks as a condition for certification.
Develop EU-aligned procurement language mandating cross-wallet compatibility tests and portable identity data models.
Organize policy-technical roundtables with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and EU bodies to harmonize expectations for fallback authentication.
review: Core tension: harmonizing national policy with EU standards while maintaining pragmatic procurement timelines; the weakness is that the options may underestimate resistance from incumbents who control platform signals and certification pathways.

name: Non-production demonstrators to prove fallback authentication feasibility
consequences: Direct effect: creates tangible evidence of viable platform-neutral paths using open standards; downstream, it can attract support and funding but may trigger expectations for production readiness that the project intentionally avoids.
options: Build two lean non-production demonstrators: a mobile-oriented FIDO2/WebAuthn path on GrapheneOS and a hardware-token-based browser fallback.
Document interoperability results, user governance, and risk assessments to inform procurement and policy decisions without implying production rollout.
Share credible lessons publicly to counter optimistic reductionism and clarify remaining gaps before formal procurement.
review: Core tension: proving feasibility without production-grade certification; the weakness is that the options do not specify a formal criteria for evaluating success or thresholds to trigger next-phase funding.

name: Resilience framing through coalition-building and political advocacy
consequences: Direct effect: shifts narrative toward resilience and continuity, unlocking political support; downstream, it risks diluting technical specifics if messaging overreaches, and may provoke strong counter-arguments about delays.
options: Publish a resilience-focused policy brief linking supplier concentration to continuity risks across critical public services and advocate concrete remedies.
Create a cross-stakeholder coalition including privacy advocates, researchers, and lawmakers to push platform-neutrality in identity infrastructure.
Map decision gates and blockers with clear timelines to accelerate adoption of fallback requirements while acknowledging political constraints.
review: Core tension: policy advocacy may outpace technical feasibility; the weakness is that the options miss a concrete plan to align budget cycles and procurement authorities across phases.

name: Standards-based open interfaces and auditable fallback pathways
consequences: Direct effect: raises transparency and interoperability; downstream, fosters external validation and reduces vendor lock-in, but may increase upfront design effort and require ongoing governance.
options: Prioritize open-standard identity APIs and require auditable interfaces with attestation and logging to demonstrate security and portability.
Sponsor independent security reviews of fallback pathways and publish findings to reduce procurement risk.
Develop and release reference implementations to showcase interoperability without vendor lock-in.
review: Core tension: balancing comprehensive auditing with timeliness of procurement; the weakness is that the options do not address governance mechanisms for ongoing maintenance of standards and ownership of open implementations.

name: Supplier concentration risk management and procurement contingency planning
consequences: Direct effect: establishes a risk-aware approach to diversification and contingency funding; downstream, it can reduce single-point failure risk but may face resistance from budget committees and incumbents used to established supply chains.
options: Develop a risk-based supplier diversification plan that allocates budget for non-Apple/Google trust-anchor alternatives and monitors concentration metrics.
Institute contingency procurement options, including staged funding and parallel contracting with fallback providers.
Implement regular risk dashboards and red-teaming exercises to identify single points of failure and trigger escalation.
review: Core tension: risk planning may lengthen engagement windows; the weakness is that the options miss a concrete method to measure concentration or trigger governance escalation in real-time.

name: Regulatory mandate for certified fallback authentication in public services
consequences: Pulling this lever creates an obligation for public portals to support a certified fallback authentication path, expanding user access resilience. A downstream implication is that agencies must update integration contracts and coordinate with standards bodies, potentially increasing upfront compliance costs and extending timelines, but reducing single-vendor risk over time.
options: Embed a regulatory requirement in public procurement guidelines that mandates a certified fallback authentication path based on open standards, with mandatory external validation and a phased rollout schedule.
Incorporate fallback-path requirements into AltID-related procurement criteria across agencies, specifying governance oversight, performance milestones, and public reporting to ensure accountability.
Create a parliamentary approval trigger for any deviations from the certified fallback path to ensure political accountability and transparent decision-making.
review: Core tension: regulatory leverage versus agency autonomy and budget constraints; weakness: the options may not fully address vendor pushback or transition costs during early rollout.

name: Open standards reference implementation for platform-neutral fallback
consequences: Pulling this lever yields a concrete, non-production blueprint for a platform-neutral fallback that demonstrates feasibility and guides procurement language. Downstream, agencies gain a reusable starting point and learning, but face integration work, potential compatibility gaps with existing MitID flows, and a moving target as standards evolve.
options: Release a non-production reference implementation of the fallback authentication pathway based on FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, designed for use by Denmark’s public portals under a permissive license.
Fund and coordinate an open-source collaboration to maintain platform-neutral verifier services, ensuring alignment with NSIS and eIDAS2 throughout lifecycle.
Adopt a policy that prohibits proprietary telemetry requirements and instead mandates independent telemetry collection for privacy-preserving fallback diagnostics.
review: Core tension: feasibility of a shared reference in a moving standards landscape; weakness: the options may assume broad participation without guaranteeing active contributors or long-term funding.

name: EU-aligned interoperability lobbying and standardization participation
consequences: Pulling this lever increases legitimacy and access to EU funding channels, and helps align procurement language with European Digital Identity Wallet expectations. But it risks slowing decision cycles and forcing compromises with EU stakeholders, potentially delaying concrete action in phase two negotiations.
options: Engage with European bodies to influence European Digital Identity Wallet materials toward platform neutrality and certified fallback pathways, seeking formal alignment in procurement language.
Sponsor a Danish public-sector standards forum to accelerate inclusion of platform-neutral criteria into AltID-related procurement documents and contracts.
Negotiate memoranda of understanding with neighboring EU member states to share best practices and reduce bilateral dependencies on specific platform vendors.
review: Core tension: EU-level consensus versus national procurement agility; weakness: the levers may depend on EU processes that have long lead times and uncertain outcomes.

name: Coalition expansion to include critical public service stakeholders
consequences: Pulling this lever broadens legitimacy and narrows informational gaps across ministries and service domains. Downstream, coordination complexity increases, risk of conflicting priorities grows, and governance overhead rises, but it strengthens alignment for procurement language and acceptance criteria.
options: Formalize a multi-stakeholder coalition including municipalities, healthcare providers, and education agencies to align on fallback acceptance criteria and procurement mapping.
Initiate targeted outreach to privacy advocates and civil-society groups to build credibility for platform-neutral approaches and risk framing.
Coordinate with the Danish Data Protection Agency to publish a public impact assessment and ensure privacy-by-design principles guide procurement language.
review: Core tension: scale and coherence across diverse actors; weakness: the options may not overcome entrenched sponsor interests or schedule constraints.

name: Economic incentives and financing strategies to de-risk platform-neutral paths
consequences: Pulling this lever creates financial mechanisms to de-risk adoption of platform-neutral fallbacks, incentivizing early demonstration work and supplier diversity. Downstream, budgets may be constrained and procurement cycles may slow to accommodate milestone-based funding, but long-term resilience improves with broader supplier participation.
options: Create a grant-and-seed fund for compliant fallback modules, with milestones tied to procurement readiness and external validation.
Negotiate phased payment terms in public contracts to align supplier incentives with early demonstration of resilience features.
Design a pre-qualification framework that lowers barriers for smaller vendors to participate in AltID interoperability efforts and platform-neutral pilots.
review: Core tension: short-term cost controls versus long-term resilience investment; weakness: financial levers may not translate into actual procurement commitments without political buy-in.

name: Independent security assurance and third-party validation framework
consequences: Pulling this lever provides external confidence in fallback approaches, increases transparency, and reduces single-actor risk. Downstream, it requires sustained funding, governance, and clear dispute-resolution processes; misalignment could slow progress or create compliance burdens.
options: Establish a standing independent security review program for fallback architectures, including annual penetration testing, code audits, and cross-agency disclosure.
Contract third-party attestations against core risk scenarios with public dashboards reporting results and corrective action timelines.
Publish vulnerability disclosure policy and a coordinated incident response plan linked to procurement cycles and contract escalation paths.
review: Core tension: external validation adds credibility but can become bureaucratic bottleneck; weakness: options may not address allocation of remediation responsibilities across agencies.

name: Procurement resilience clauses for platform-neutral authentication pilots
consequences: Embedding resilience clauses creates a governance pathway for platform-neutral tests, increasing procurement transparency and multi-ministry coordination. Downstream, this can slow procurement cycles and raise coordination overhead, but it reduces the risk of vendor lock-in if pilots demonstrate value and are well-governed.
options: Draft procurement amendments requiring phased pilots of platform-neutral fallback authentication within AltID contracts, including concrete milestones for testing and sunset clauses.
Negotiate resilience sub-clauses in ministry contracts that mandate independent testing environments and third-party assessments of fallback pathways prior to any production deployment.
Establish a cross-ministry framework to fund and govern platform-neutral authentication pilots, sharing risks, governance responsibilities, and evaluation criteria across agencies.
review: Core tension centers on balancing rapid procurement cycles with rigorous pilot validation; a weakness the options miss is aligning cross-ministry data governance and consent across agencies.

name: Independent cross-border trust anchors for fallback authentication
consequences: Introducing independent trust anchors reduces single-vendor dependency and diversifies trust signals. Downstream, governance and liability responsibilities rise, and cross-border recognition of anchors may face regulatory and legal alignment challenges across jurisdictions.
options: Formalize agreements with independent attestation bodies to serve as platform-neutral trust anchors for OpenID Connect and WebAuthn flows.
Establish a government-backed hardware token program as a durable fallback anchor interoperable with multiple major ecosystems.
Create a market-access pathway for certified fallback providers to integrate with AltID via a shared assurance framework.
review: Core tension centers on reliance on independent anchors raising liability and interoperability questions; a weakness is potential lack of binding legal recognition across EU and uncertain funding commitments.

name: Open-source verifier stack governance for platform-neutral access
consequences: An open-source verifier stack promotes transparency and interoperability, reducing vendor lock-in. Downstream, there is risk of fragmented governance and uneven participation if sponsors are not clearly mandated or resourced.
options: Sponsor an open-source verifier stack implementing FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, with formal security reviews and a bug-bounty program.
Publish a reference implementation under a permissive license with governance and contribution guidelines to encourage broader participation.
Create a certified build pipeline and reproducible test vectors to support government audits and external verification.
review: Core tension is open-source governance versus public sector procurement hurdles; a weakness is potential uneven participation and lack of mandated sponsor to drive timely decisions.

name: Danish-EU interoperability roadmap linking AltID to European Wallet standards
consequences: Aligning national efforts with European Wallet standards reduces cross-border friction and positions Denmark within EU digital identity momentum. Downstream, this may slow domestic timing due to EU negotiation cycles and require compromises in national procurement pacing.
options: Lead a joint Danish-EU task force to map AltID requirements to European Wallet specifications and publish a concrete forward plan.
Push for formal recognition of platform-neutral fallback within NSIS risk-management guidelines across member states and regulators.
Develop a concurrent EU funding application to pilot cross-border fallback access between AltID and eIDAS2 wallets.
review: Core tension involves EU alignment cycles versus domestic procurement tempo; a weakness is over-reliance on EU standards potentially delaying tangible domestic pilots.

name: Regulatory sandbox for platform-neutral fallback testing
consequences: A sandbox enables controlled experimentation with fallback paths without full production deployment. Downstream, scope creep and misaligned incentives could arise if exit criteria and governance are not clearly defined.
options: Establish a regulatory sandbox with DMCE and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to enable controlled testing of fallback authentication without production deployment.
Create a time-bound pilot program across ministries with measurable KPIs for resilience, privacy, and performance.
Engage civil-society and privacy groups as observers to ensure governance transparency and public trust while testing.
review: Core tension is sandbox risk of scope creep; a weakness is that sandbox outcomes may not translate quickly into procurement language or policy changes.

name: Privacy-by-design in fallback authentication workflows
consequences: Embedding privacy-by-design reduces risk to public trust and regulatory exposure while improving accountability. Downstream, there may be increased design and review costs and potential impact on user experience if privacy controls are overly strict.
options: Institute privacy-by-design reviews for all fallback authentication flows, emphasizing data minimization, consent controls, and auditability.
Develop risk-based authentication models that minimize data transfer to platform providers and limit telemetry exposure.
Publish a publicly accessible privacy impact assessment and red-team findings to enhance citizen confidence and accountability.
review: Core tension is balancing strong privacy controls with user experience and performance requirements; a weakness is that privacy measures alone may not address broader resilience or procurement constraints.
